rulings.Case Title: ITC FINANCIAL LICENSES, INC. VS. STAN SCHULDINER
rulings.Calendar Matter: Buyer'S Notice Of Motion To Tax Costs, And Amend The Judgment Language That Says Bancorp/Itc Prevailed On All Issues; Memo
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Friday, May 31, 2019, line 1. RESPONDENT STAN SCHULDINER'S Motion To Tax Costs, And Amend The Judgment Language That Says Bancorp/Itc Prevailed On All Issues. The hearing will be at 9am, not 9:30am. Cross-complainant Stan Schuldiner's motion to tax costs and amend the judgment is granted in part. The December 21, 2018 memorandum of costs filed by cross-defendants ITC Financial Licenses, Inc. and The Bancorp Bank claims filing and motion fees of $1,620.70, deposition costs of $5,816.51 and total costs of $7,437.21, which, absent a timely filed amended memorandum, are the maximum amounts of costs recoverable by ITC and Bancorp. There was no timely filed amended memorandum. Assuming without deciding that the change in the website language constitutes recovery "other than monetary relief" by Mr. Schuldiner within the meaning of CCP 1032(a)(4), the court exercises its discretion per that subsection to determine that ITC and Bancorp are the prevailing parties for purposes of recovery of costs because the primary relief sought by Mr. Schuldiner was monetary, not injunctive. ITC and Bancorp have shown that they reasonably and necessarily incurred at least $1,620.70 in filing and motion fees and $4,969.47 in deposition costs for a total of $6,590.17, which will be added to the judgment in their favor. Mr. Schuldiner has not shown that he is entitled to an amended judgment stating that he is the prevailing party for any purpose due to the change in the website language and its mooting his request for injunctive relief. Mr. Schuldiner has not shown that he made a pre-litigation request that the website language be changed, so he is not able to avail himself of the catalyst theory. There is no other legal theory that would entitle Mr. Schuldiner to be declared a prevailing party in this litigation. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for ITC and Bancorp is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The hearing will be at 9am, not 9:30am. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: MARGARET K REYES VS. RICHARD CURCI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Friday, May 31, 2019, line 2. RESPONDENT RICHARD CURCI'S Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses And Request For Sanctions. The judge pro tem's well-reasoned report and recommendations are adopted and respondents' motion to compel further discovery responses and for sanctions is GRANTED. Petitioner Margaret Reyes is ordered to serve complete, responsive, straightforward and verified responses to respondents': (a) special interrogatories 1-6, 8-9, 11-15, 17-19; (b) requests for admission 1-3, 5-6, 12, 14-19, 21, 23-24, 26-29; and (c) form interrogatories 2.5, 12.6, 17.1. Sanctions of $1,250 shall be paid by petitioner to respondents' counsel under CCP §2023.030. The further discovery responses are due, and the sanctions shall be paid, within 10 days of notice of entry of this order. rulings.Case Title: DAVID CRUZ VS. BAY AREA GREEN CROSS DISPENSARY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motions To Compel Defendants Further Responses To Requests For Admissions, Requests For Production Of Documents, And Special Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 31, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF DAVID CRUZ'S Motions To Compel Defendants Further Responses To Requests For Admissions, Requests For Production Of Documents, And Special Interrogatories Pro Tem Judge Rebecca Woodson, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff DAVID CRUZ's Motion to Compel Defendant BAY AREA GREEN CROSS DISPENSARY, INC.'S Further Responses to Plaintiff's First Set of Requests for Admission is GRANTED as to Request for Admission No. 4, and is DENIED as to Requests for Admission Nos. 3, 9, 11. Plaintiff DAVID CRUZ's Motion to Compel Defendant BAY AREA GREEN CROSS DISPENSARY, INC.'s Further Responses to Plaintiff's Second Set of Requests for Production of Documents is UNDER SUBMISSION as to Requests for Production Nos. 35, 36, and is DENIED as to Requests for Production Nos. 42, 47. The Court will rule on opposing party's objections from the bench. Plaintiff DAVID CRUZ's Motion to Compel Defendant BAY AREA GREEN CROSS DISPENSARY, INC.'s Responses to Plaintiff's First Set of Special Interrogatories is DENIED as to Special Interrogatory No. 6, and is GRANTED as to Special Interrogatory No. 8. The Court will rule on opposing party's objections from the bench. Plaintiff DAVID CRUZ's Motion to Compel Defendant CITISHIELD, INC.'s Further Responses to Plaintiff's First Set of Requests for Admission is DENIED as to Requests for Admission Nos. 9, 11. The Court will rule on opposing party's objections from the bench. Plaintiff DAVID CRUZ's Motion to Compel Defendant REED's Further Responses to Plaintiff's First Set of Requests for Production is GRANTED as to Request for Production No. 12. Further, Plaintiff DAVID CRUZ's Motion to Compel Defendant REED's Further Responses to Plaintiff's Second Set of Requests for Production is GRANTED as to Request for Production No. 28 subject to the terms and conditions of the Protective Order in this matter, executed by the parties on August 14, 2018 and October 2, 2018 respectively. The Court will rule on opposing party's objections from the bench. The parties are ordered to meet and confer prior to hearing on the matter in order to attempt to resolve any outstanding discovery issues, and report to the court as to the parties' progress, if any. (end of tentative ruling part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: FELISIA THIBODEAUX VS. BAYVIEW HUNTERS POINT MULTIPURPOSE SENIOR SERVICES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Bayview Hunters Point Multipurpose Senior Services And Cathy Davis' Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Friday, May 31, 2019, line 10. Defendants Bayview Hunters Point Multipurpose Senior Services and Cathy Davis' summary judgment motion is DENIED. An adverse employment action is an essential element of each of plaintiff Felisia Thibodeaux's claims and thus is the focus of defendants' motion. Plaintiff's supposed inability to prove causation was argued for the first time in reply, so that argument is not considered. A plain reading of plaintiff's resignation letter indicates she resigned. (Davis. Dec. Ex. A.) However, while plaintiff's evidence is weak, it must be construed liberally. (Binder v. Aetna Life Ins. Co. (1999) 75 Cal.App.4th 832, 838.) And the deposition excerpts combine with the letter to create triable issues of fact on adverse employment action and constructive discharge. There are, for instance, disputes as to whether plaintiff's efforts to investigate Frank Williams and protect Shereena Abdullah resulted in plaintiff being subjected to retaliation and a hostile work environment by Davis. Examples include undermining plaintiff's authority, hostile interactions and the termination of plaintiff's direct report, Jose Reyes. (UMF 23, 25-26, 28, 31, 40, 42-43; Davis Dec. Ex. A.) While isolated social slights and exhibitions of anger may not on their own establish retaliation, a pattern of such acts, combined with efforts to undermine plaintiff's functioning in the workplace, may suffice. (Yanowitz v. L'Oreal USA, Inc. (2005) 36 Cal.4th 1028, 1053-54, 1056.) It is for a finder of fact to decide whether Davis retaliated and whether plaintiff's resignation letter was motivated by intolerable work conditions reaching the level of an adverse employment action. Finally, constructive discharge is alleged in the complaint (26), so it provides a basis for plaintiff's allegations of adverse employment action. While not pled as its own cause of action, constructive discharge supports the retaliation and wrongful termination claims as it may amount to termination as a matter of law. (Turner v. Anheuser-Busch, Inc. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 1238, 1244-45; Steele v. Youthful Offender Parole Bd. (2008) 162 Cal.App.4th 1241, 1253.) Unlike in Featherstone v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (2017) 10 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1161, there is evidence to suggest that employer misconduct prompted plaintiff to resign. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MOTIVA ENTERPRISES LLC A DELAWRE LIMITED LIABILITY VS. AXIS SURPLUS INSURANCE COMPANY AN ILLINOIS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order To Seal Motions For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Friday, May 31, 2019, line 9. PLAINTIFF MOTIVA ENTERPRISES LLC'S Motion For Order To Seal Motions For Summary Judgment. Plaintiff Motiva Enterprises, LLC's motion to seal is GRANTED. The court will execute Motiva's proposed order. The court finds good cause to seal the adduced documents. The court did not rely on those documents to render its prior ruling that took the motions for summary judgment off calendar. There is no public right of access to documents that the court does not rely upon to render its decision. (See Mercury Interactive Corp. v. Klein (2007) 158 Cal.App.4th 60.) The court notes that if the parties re-new their motions for summary judgment/adjudication and lodge documents provisionally under seal, they will need to comply with the dictates of CRC 2.550 et seq. The court rejects Axis and Endurance's timeliness argument. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/RBU)

If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant and Cross-Complainants BRY1890 LLC and MRY101 LLC's Motion to Compel is GRANTED. Plaintiff/Cross-Defendant LUIGI DI RUOCCO shall serve verified responses to Plaintiff's Form Interrogatories, Set One, by 5:00 p.m. PST June 24, 2019. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is GRANTED. Moving party to submit Order to the Court for signature. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rebecca.woodson@dentons.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
